chapter.subject: Biology
chapter.name: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production

question.text: Which of the following is considered the "Father of the Green Revolution" in the world?
question.explanation: Norman E. Borlaug, an American agronomist, is considered the father of the Green Revolution worldwide for his work in developing semi-dwarf, high-yielding varieties of wheat. M.S. Swaminathan is the father of the Green Revolution in India.

question.text: The term "totipotency" refers to the ability of:
question.option: A single cell to divide and produce all of the differentiated cells in an organism.
An animal to regenerate lost parts.
A plant to resist viral infections.
question.explanation: Totipotency is the capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant. This property is heavily utilized in plant tissue culture to rapidly produce thousands of plants.

question.text: Sonalika and Kalyan Sona are high yielding and disease-resistant varieties of:
question.explanation: In 1963, several varieties such as Sonalika and Kalyan Sona, which were high yielding and disease resistant, were introduced all over the wheat-growing belt of India.

question.text: Which of the following is an example of a freshwater fish widely cultured in India?
question.explanation: Some of the freshwater fishes which are very common include Catla, Rohu and common carp. Hilsa, Sardines, Mackerel and Pomfrets are some of the marine fishes that are eaten.

question.text: The management of animals for milk and its products for human consumption is called:
question.explanation: Dairying is the management of animals for milk and its products for human consumption.

question.text: Which of the following refers to the rearing of honey bees?
question.explanation: Bee-keeping or apiculture is the maintenance of hives of honeybees for the production of honey and beeswax.

question.text: What is the main objective of animal breeding?
question.option: To increase the number of wild animals
To increase the yield of animals and improve the desirable qualities of the produce
question.explanation: Animal breeding aims at increasing the yield of animals and improving the desirable qualities of the produce.

question.text: Which of the following is NOT a freshwater fish?
question.explanation: Catla, Rohu and common carp are freshwater fishes. Mackerel, Hilsa, Sardines and Pomfrets are marine fishes.

question.text: The agricultural practice of breeding and raising livestock is called:
question.explanation: Animal husbandry is the agricultural practice of breeding and raising livestock.

question.text: The term 'Apiculture' refers to the rearing of:
question.explanation: Bee-keeping or apiculture is the maintenance of hives of honeybees for the production of honey.

question.text: Which of the following is the most common species of honey bee reared in India?
question.explanation: There are several species of honeybees which can be reared. Of these, the most common species is Apis indica.

question.text: The management of animals for milk and its products for human consumption is called:
question.explanation: Dairying is the management of animals for milk and its products for human consumption.

question.text: Which of the following viral diseases affects poultry?
question.explanation: Poultry refers to domesticated birds like chickens, ducks, turkeys and geese. They are susceptible to several viral, bacterial and fungal diseases. Ranikhet disease (Newcastle disease) is a very common and highly infectious viral disease of poultry.

question.text: Which of the following is a marine fish commonly consumed in India?
question.explanation: Freshwater fishes which are very common include Catla, Rohu and common carp. Some of the marine fishes that are eaten include - Hilsa, Sardines, Mackerel and Pomfrets.

question.text: The Blue Revolution is associated with a significant increase in the production of:
question.explanation: Aquaculture and pisciculture have been able to increase the production of aquatic plants and animals, both fresh-water and marine. This has led to the development and flourishing of the fishery industry, and it has brought a lot of income to the farmers in particular and the country in general. We now talk about 'Blue Revolution' as being implemented along the same lines as 'Green Revolution'.

question.text: The process of crossing two different plant varieties, each having a specific desired character, to get a single variety with both desired characters is called:
question.explanation: Cross hybridisation among the selected parents: The desired characters have very often to be combined from two different plants (parents), for example high protein quality of one parent may need to be combined with disease resistance from another parent. This is possible by cross hybridising the two parents to produce hybrids that genetically combine the desired characters in one plant.

question.text: Which crop variety is resistant to the white rust disease?
question.explanation: Pusa Swarnim (Karan rai) is a variety of Brassica that is bred by hybridisation and selection for resistance to white rust disease.

question.text: Pusa Komal is a disease-resistant variety of which crop?
question.explanation: Pusa Komal is a variety of Cowpea that is resistant to the bacterial blight disease.

question.text: Which of the following describes animal husbandry?
question.option: The agricultural practice of cultivating crops
The agricultural practice of breeding and raising livestock
The industrial processing of animal products
The management of wild animals in sanctuaries
question.explanation: Animal husbandry is the agricultural practice of breeding and raising livestock. As such it is a vital skill for farmers and is as much science as it is art. Animal husbandry deals with the care and breeding of livestock like buffaloes, cows, pigs, horses, cattle, sheep, camels, goats, etc.

question.text: It is estimated that more than 70 per cent of the world livestock population is in:
question.explanation: It is estimated that more than 70 per cent of the world livestock population is in India and China. However, it is surprising to note that the contribution to the world farm produce is only 25 per cent, i.e., the productivity per unit is very low.

question.text: Dairy management deals with processes and systems that increase yield and improve quality of milk. Milk yield is primarily dependent on the:
question.option: Quality of breeds in the farm
question.explanation: Dairying is the management of animals for milk and its products for human consumption. Milk yield is primarily dependent on the quality of breeds in the farm. Selection of good breeds having high yielding potential (under the climatic conditions of the area), combined with resistance to diseases is very important.

question.text: Which of the following is an example of an infectious disease that can spread through contaminated food?
question.explanation: Salmonella typhi is a pathogenic bacterium which causes typhoid fever in human beings. These pathogens generally enter the small intestine through food and water contaminated with them.

question.text: Fish, shell-fish, prawns, and crabs are caught, processed, or sold in which industry?
question.explanation: Fishery is an industry devoted to the catching, processing or selling of fish, shellfish or other aquatic animals. A large number of our population is dependent on fish, fish products and other aquatic animals such as prawn, crab, lobster, edible oyster, etc., for food.

question.text: Which of the following is a common freshwater fish?
question.explanation: Some of the freshwater fishes which are very common include Catla, Rohu and common carp. Some of the marine fishes that are eaten include – Hilsa, Sardines, Mackerel and Pomfrets.

question.text: In India, agriculture accounts for approximately what percentage of the country's GDP?
question.explanation: India is mainly an agricultural country. Agriculture accounts for approximately 33 per cent of India's GDP and employs nearly 62 per cent of the population.

question.text: What percentage of the Indian population is employed by agriculture?
question.explanation: Agriculture accounts for approximately 33 per cent of India's GDP and employs nearly 62 per cent of the population.

question.text: Which of the following is a semi-dwarf variety of wheat developed by Nobel laureate Norman E. Borlaug?
question.option: The question asks for the creator, but Kalyan Sona and Sonalika were derived from Borlaug's varieties.
question.explanation: Nobel laureate Norman E. Borlaug, at International Centre for Wheat and Maize Improvement in Mexico, developed semi-dwarf wheat. In 1963, several varieties such as Sonalika and Kalyan Sona, which were high yielding and disease resistant, were introduced all over the wheat-growing belt of India.

question.text: Which of the following methods is conventionally used for breeding disease resistance in plants?
question.explanation: Breeding is carried out by the conventional breeding techniques (described earlier) or by mutation breeding. The conventional method of breeding for disease resistance is that of hybridisation and selection.

question.text: Which wheat variety was developed to be resistant to leaf and stripe rust and hill bunt?
question.explanation: Wheat variety Himgiri is resistant to Leaf and stripe rust, hill bunt.

question.text: The Brassica variety Pusa swarnim (Karan rai) was bred for resistance against which disease?
question.explanation: Brassica variety Pusa swarnim (Karan rai) is resistant to White rust.

question.text: Another major cause for large scale destruction of crop plants and crop produce is insect and pest infestation. Insect resistance in host crop plants may be due to morphological, biochemical, or physiological characteristics. Hairy leaves in several plants are associated with resistance to which pests?
question.option: Jassids in cotton and cereal leaf beetle in wheat
question.explanation: Insect resistance in host crop plants may be due to morphological, biochemical or physiological characteristics. Hairy leaves in several plants are associated with resistance to insect pests, e.g, resistance to jassids in cotton and cereal leaf beetle in wheat.

question.text: In wheat, solid stems lead to non-preference by which pest?
question.explanation: In wheat, solid stems lead to non-preference by the stem sawfly.

question.text: Smooth leaved and nectar-less cotton varieties do not attract which common pest?
question.explanation: Smooth leaved and nectar-less cotton varieties do not attract bollworms.

question.text: Which wheat variety, developed in 2000, has a high protein content and has been used as a donor for improving cultivated wheat?
question.explanation: Wheat variety, Atlas 66, having a high protein content, has been used as a donor for improving cultivated wheat.

question.text: It has been possible to develop an iron-fortified rice variety containing over how many times as much iron as in commonly consumed varieties?
question.explanation: It has been possible to develop an iron-fortified rice variety containing over five times as much iron as in commonly consumed varieties.

question.text: The Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI) in New Delhi has released several vegetable crops rich in vitamins and minerals. Which vitamin is enriched in their bitter gourd, bathua, mustard, and tomato varieties?
question.explanation: The Indian Agricultural Research Institute, New Delhi has also released several vegetable crops that are rich in vitamins and minerals, e.g., Vitamin A enriched carrots, spinach, pumpkin; Vitamin C enriched bitter gourd, bathua, mustard, tomato...

question.text: Mating of closely related individuals within the same breed for 4-6 generations is called:
question.explanation: When breeding is between animals of the same breed for 4-6 generations, it is called inbreeding. Continued inbreeding, especially close inbreeding, usually reduces fertility and even productivity (inbreeding depression).

question.text: Which of the following techniques is employed to produce virus-free plants from an infected plant?
question.explanation: Even if the plant is infected with a virus, the meristem (apical and axillary) is free of virus. Hence, one can remove the meristem and grow it in vitro to obtain virus-free plants.

question.text: Pomato is a somatic hybrid produced by the fusion of protoplasts of:
question.explanation: Isolated protoplasts from two different varieties of plants can be fused to get hybrid protoplasts, called somatic hybrids. Fusion of protoplasts of tomato and potato resulted in a new plant, the pomato.

question.text: The technique of Multiple Ovulation Embryo Transfer Technology (MOET) is primarily used for:
question.explanation: MOET is a programme for herd improvement. In this method, a cow is administered hormones, with FSH-like activity, to induce follicular maturation and super ovulation. The embryos at 8-32 cell stages, are recovered non-surgically and transferred to surrogate mothers.

question.text: Which mating method is used to combine desirable qualities of two different breeds into a single progeny?
question.explanation: In cross-breeding, superior males of one breed are mated with superior females of another breed. Cross-breeding allows the desirable qualities of two different breeds to be combined.

question.text: The mule is a result of:
question.explanation: In interspecific hybridization, male and female animals of two different related species are mated. For example, a mule is produced from a cross between a male donkey and a female horse.

question.text: Which of the following is a breed of cattle?
question.explanation: Jersey is a breed of cattle (cow). Leghorn and Rhode Island Red are poultry breeds, and Hisardale is a sheep breed.

question.text: To overcome 'inbreeding depression', which of the following practices is recommended?
question.option: A single outcross with an unrelated superior animal of the same breed
Mating with a completely different species
Isolating the herd from all other animals
question.explanation: It often helps to overcome inbreeding depression by a single outcross. This means mating the selected animals with unrelated superior animals of the same breed (having no common ancestors for 4-6 generations).

question.text: Inbreeding refers to the mating of:
question.option: More closely related individuals within the same breed for 4-6 generations
Unrelated animals of the same breed
question.explanation: When breeding is between animals of the same breed it is called inbreeding. Inbreeding refers to the mating of more closely related individuals within the same breed for 4-6 generations.

question.text: Which of the following is a severe consequence of continued close inbreeding?
question.explanation: Continued inbreeding, especially close inbreeding, usually reduces fertility and even productivity. This is called inbreeding depression.

question.text: The term "Green Revolution" is associated with a dramatic increase in the production of:
question.option: Food crops like wheat and rice
question.explanation: Green Revolution was dependent to a large extent on plant breeding techniques for development of high-yielding and disease resistant varieties in wheat, rice, maize, etc.

question.text: Sonalika and Kalyan Sona are high yielding varieties of:
question.explanation: In 1963, several varieties such as Sonalika and Kalyan Sona, which were high yielding and disease resistant, were introduced all over the wheat-growing belt of India.

question.text: In animal breeding, the term 'inbreeding' refers to:
question.option: Mating of related individuals of the same breed for 4-6 generations
Mating of unrelated individuals of the same breed
Mating of individuals of different breeds
Mating of individuals of different species
question.explanation: When breeding is between animals of the same breed it is called inbreeding, while crosses between different breeds are called outbreeding. Inbreeding refers to the mating of more closely related individuals within the same breed for 4-6 generations.

question.text: Continued inbreeding, especially close inbreeding, usually reduces fertility and even productivity. This is called:
question.explanation: However, continued inbreeding, especially close inbreeding, usually reduces fertility and even productivity. This is called inbreeding depression.

question.text: Hisardale is a new breed of sheep developed in Punjab by crossing Bikaneri ewes and Marino rams. This is an example of:
question.explanation: In this method, superior males of one breed are mated with superior females of another breed. Cross-breeding allows the desirable qualities of two different breeds to be combined. Hisardale is a new breed of sheep developed in Punjab by crossing Bikaneri ewes and Marino rams.

question.text: Which of the following methods is used to improve the chances of successful production of hybrids by administering hormones like FSH to induce super-ovulation?
question.explanation: Multiple Ovulation Embryo Transfer Technology (MOET) is one such programme for herd improvement. In this method, a cow is administered hormones, with FSH-like activity, to induce follicular maturation and super ovulation - instead of one egg, which they normally yield per cycle, they produce 6-8 eggs.

question.text: What is the main advantage of artificial insemination in animal breeding?
question.option: It allows semen from a single superior male to be used to impregnate many females
It guarantees the birth of a male offspring
It prevents the spread of genetic diseases completely
It always results in multiple births
question.explanation: Controlled breeding experiments are carried out using artificial insemination. The semen is collected from the male that is chosen as a parent and injected into the reproductive tract of the selected female by the breeder. Semen from one superior male can be used to inseminate multiple females, and it can also be frozen and transported.

question.text: In the Multiple Ovulation Embryo Transfer (MOET) technique, the fertilised eggs are recovered and transferred to surrogate mothers at which cellular stage?
question.explanation: In MOET, a cow is administered hormones to induce super ovulation. The animal is either mated with an elite bull or artificially inseminated. The fertilised eggs at 8-32 cells stages, are recovered non-surgically and transferred to surrogate mothers.

question.text: The entire collection of plants/seeds having all the diverse alleles for all genes in a given crop is called:
question.explanation: The entire collection (of plants/seeds) having all the diverse alleles for all genes in a given crop is called germplasm collection. This is the root of any breeding programme.

question.text: Taichung Native-1, from which high-yielding semi-dwarf rice varieties were derived, was developed in which country?
question.explanation: Semi-dwarf rice varieties were derived from IR-8, (developed at International Rice Research Institute (IRRI), Philippines) and Taichung Native-1 (from Taiwan). The derivatives were introduced in 1966.

question.text: Resistance to yellow mosaic virus in bhindi (Abelmoschus esculentus) was transferred from a wild species and resulted in a new variety called:
question.explanation: Resistance to yellow mosaic virus in bhindi (Abelmoschus esculentus) was transferred from a wild species and resulted in a new variety of A. esculentus called Parbhani kranti.

question.text: In mung bean, resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew was induced by:
question.explanation: It is possible to induce mutations artificially through use of chemicals or radiations (like gamma radiations), and selecting and using the plants that have the desirable character as a source in breeding - this process is called mutation breeding. In mung bean, resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew were induced by mutations.

question.text: Smooth leaved and nectar-less cotton varieties do not attract which insect pests?
question.explanation: Some morphological, biochemical or physiological characteristics give the plants resistance to insects. Smooth leaved and nectar-less cotton varieties do not attract bollworms.

question.text: High aspartic acid, low nitrogen and sugar content in maize leads to resistance to which pest?
question.explanation: High aspartic acid, low nitrogen and sugar content in maize leads to resistance to maize stem borers.

question.text: What does the term 'breed' refer to in animal husbandry?
question.option: A group of animals found in the same geographical area
A group of animals related by descent and similar in most characters
Any two animals that can interbreed naturally
Animals that share the same diet
question.explanation: A group of animals related by descent and similar in most characters like general appearance, features, size, configuration, etc., are said to belong to a breed.

question.text: When breeding is between animals of the same breed it is called inbreeding, while crosses between different breeds are called:
question.explanation: When breeding is between animals of the same breed it is called inbreeding, while crosses between different breeds are called outbreeding.

question.text: Inbreeding depression, which is a reduction in fertility and productivity, is caused by:
question.explanation: However, continued inbreeding, especially close inbreeding, usually reduces fertility and even productivity. This is called inbreeding depression.

question.text: The practice of mating of animals within the same breed, but having no common ancestors on either side of their pedigree up to 4-6 generations, is called:
question.explanation: Out-crossing: This is the practice of mating of animals within the same breed, but having no common ancestors on either side of their pedigree up to 4-6 generations. The offspring of such a mating is known as an out-cross.

question.text: The development and flourishing of the fishery industry is closely associated with which revolution?
question.explanation: Fisheries has an important place in Indian economy. It provides income and employment to millions of fishermen and farmers, particularly in the coastal states. For many, it is the only source of their livelihood. In order to meet the increasing demands on fisheries, different techniques have been applied to increase catch. Through aquaculture and pisciculture we have been able to increase the production of aquatic plants and animals, both fresh-water and marine. This has led to the development and flourishing of the fishery industry, and it has brought a lot of income to the farmers in particular and the country in general. We now talk about 'Blue Revolution' as being implemented along the same lines as 'Green Revolution'.

question.text: Traditional farming can only yield a limited biomass. What practice helped increase crop yields to meet the demands of a growing human population?
question.explanation: Traditional farming can only yield a limited biomass, as food for humans and animals. Better management practices and increase in acreage can increase yield, but only to a limited extent. Plant breeding as a technology has helped increase yields to a very large extent.

question.text: The purposeful manipulation of plant species in order to create desired plant types that are better suited for cultivation, give better yields and are disease resistant is known as:
question.explanation: Plant breeding is the purposeful manipulation of plant species in order to create desired plant types that are better suited for cultivation, give better yields and are disease resistant.

question.text: What was the phase of dramatically increased agricultural production in India called?
question.explanation: Who in India has not heard of Green Revolution which was responsible for our country to not merely meet the national requirements in food production but also helped us even to export it? Green revolution was dependent to a large extent on plant breeding techniques for development of high-yielding and disease resistant varieties in wheat, rice, maize, etc.

question.text: Which varieties of semi-dwarf wheat were introduced in India in 1963 that were high yielding and disease resistant?
question.explanation: In 1963, several varieties such as Sonalika and Kalyan Sona, which were high yielding and disease resistant, were introduced all over the wheat-growing belt of India.

question.text: Semi-dwarf rice varieties IR-8 and Taichung Native-1 were derived from which locations respectively?
question.explanation: Semi-dwarf rice varieties were derived from IR-8, (developed at International Rice Research Institute (IRRI), Philippines) and Taichung Native-1 (from Taiwan).

question.text: Later in India, better-yielding semi-dwarf rice varieties were developed. Two prominent examples are:
question.option: Pusa shubhra and Pusa Snowball K-1
question.explanation: Later better-yielding semi-dwarf varieties Jaya and Ratna were developed in India.

question.text: Which sugarcane species was originally grown in North India, but had poor sugar content and yield?
question.explanation: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield.

question.text: Which cauliflower varieties were developed to resist black rot and curl blight black rot?
question.option: Pusa shubhra and Pusa Snowball K-1
question.explanation: Cauliflower varieties Pusa shubhra and Pusa Snowball K-1 are resistant to Black rot and Curl blight black rot.

question.text: The cowpea variety Pusa Komal was developed for resistance against which bacterial disease?
question.explanation: Cowpea variety Pusa Komal is resistant to Bacterial blight.

question.text: Which disease is the chilli variety Pusa Sadabahar resistant to?
question.option: Chilly mosaic virus, Tobacco mosaic virus, and Leaf curl
question.explanation: Chilli variety Pusa Sadabahar is resistant to Chilly mosaic virus, Tobacco mosaic virus and Leaf curl.

question.text: A major limitation of using conventional breeding for disease resistance is:
question.option: The limited number of disease resistance genes available in the crop varieties and wild relatives
It takes too much land to perform
question.explanation: Conventional breeding is often constrained by the availability of limited number of disease resistance genes that are present and identified in various crop varieties or wild relatives.

question.text: Maize varieties with high aspartic acid, low nitrogen, and sugar content exhibit resistance to which pest?
question.explanation: High aspartic acid, low nitrogen and sugar content in maize leads to resistance to maize stem borers.

question.text: The Brassica (rapeseed mustard) variety Pusa Gaurav was bred for resistance against which pest?
question.explanation: Brassica (rapeseed mustard) variety Pusa Gaurav is resistant to Aphids.

question.text: Which flat bean varieties were developed to resist Jassids, aphids, and fruit borer?
question.explanation: Flat bean varieties Pusa Sem 2 and Pusa Sem 3 are resistant to Jassids, aphids and fruit borer.

question.text: The Okra (Bhindi) varieties Pusa Sawani and Pusa A-4 are resistant to which insects?
question.explanation: Okra (Bhindi) varieties Pusa Sawani and Pusa A-4 are resistant to Shoot and Fruit borer.

question.text: Which IARI released vegetable crops are specifically mentioned as being enriched with Vitamin A?
question.explanation: Vitamin A enriched carrots, spinach, pumpkin...

question.text: Which minerals are enriched in the IARI released spinach and bathua varieties?
question.explanation: ...iron and calcium enriched spinach and bathua...

question.text: Which IARI released crops are protein enriched?
question.option: Broad lablab, French, and garden peas
question.explanation: ...and protein enriched broad lablab, French and garden peas.

question.text: Traditional farming can only yield a limited biomass. What is the alternative that is being considered as an alternate source of proteins for animal and human nutrition?
question.explanation: One of the alternate sources of proteins for animal and human nutrition is Single Cell Protein (SCP).

question.text: Which of the following crosses led to the development of the new sheep breed "Hisardale" in Punjab?
question.explanation: Hisardale is a new breed of sheep developed in Punjab by crossing Bikaneri ewes and Marino rams. This is an example of cross-breeding, where superior males of one breed are mated with superior females of another breed.

question.text: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. It was successfully crossed with which tropical cane to get the desirable qualities?
question.explanation: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. Tropical canes grown in south India Saccharum officinarum had thicker stems and higher sugar content. These two species were successfully crossed to get sugar cane varieties combining the desirable qualities.

question.text: Biofortification refers to:
question.option: The use of microbes to extract heavy metals from soil.
Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats.
The artificial addition of nutrients to food during processing.
The fortification of natural habitats to protect endangered species.
question.explanation: Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats is the most practical means to improve public health. This process is called biofortification. Examples include iron-fortified rice and vitamin A enriched carrots.

question.text: During plant tissue culture, what determines whether the explant differentiates into roots or shoots?
question.option: The temperature of the culture room
The concentration and ratio of auxins and cytokinins
The amount of sucrose in the medium
question.explanation: The nutrient medium must provide a carbon source such as sucrose and also inorganic salts, vitamins, amino acids and growth regulators like auxins, cytokinins etc. The ratio of auxins to cytokinins determines whether roots or shoots will develop.

question.text: Which of the following statements about Single Cell Protein (SCP) is FALSE?
question.option: It is an alternate source of proteins for animal and human nutrition.
Microbes like Spirulina can be grown easily to produce large quantities of protein.
Methylophilus methylotrophus can produce 25 tonnes of protein.
It requires large tracts of fertile agricultural land to produce.
question.explanation: SCP reduces the pressure on agricultural land. Microbes like Spirulina can be grown easily on materials like waste water from potato processing plants, straw, molasses, animal manure and even sewage.

question.text: Which of the following crop varieties is specifically bred for resistance to White rust?
question.explanation: Pusa Swarnim (Karan rai), a variety of Brassica, was bred for resistance to the disease White rust.

question.text: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. It was crossed with a south Indian species to get desirable qualities. What was the south Indian species?
question.explanation: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. Tropical canes grown in south India Saccharum officinarum had thicker stems and higher sugar content but did not grow well in north India. These two species were successfully crossed.

question.text: Which of the following plant parts is generally free of viruses and is therefore used as an explant in tissue culture to recover healthy plants from diseased ones?
question.explanation: Even if the plant is infected with a virus, the meristem (apical and axillary) is free of virus. Hence, one can remove the meristem and grow it in vitro to obtain virus-free plants.

question.text: The capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called:
question.explanation: The capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency. This property is extensively utilized in plant tissue culture.

question.text: The green revolution in India was highly dependent on plant breeding techniques. Who is known as the Father of the Green Revolution in India?
question.explanation: The Green Revolution in India was led by Dr. M. S. Swaminathan, known as the 'Father of the Green Revolution in India' for his role in introducing and further developing high-yielding varieties of wheat in India.

question.text: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. It was crossed with a species from south India that had thicker stems and higher sugar content. Which species was it?
question.explanation: Saccharum barberi was originally grown in north India, but had poor sugar content and yield. Tropical canes grown in south India Saccharum officinarum had thicker stems and higher sugar content but did not grow well in north India. These two species were successfully crossed to get sugar cane varieties combining the desirable qualities of high yield, thick stems, high sugar and ability to grow in the sugar cane areas of north India.

question.text: Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats, is the most practical means to improve public health. This process is called:
question.explanation: Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats - is the most practical means to improve public health. This process is called biofortification.

question.text: Spirulina is a microbe that can be grown on a large scale on materials like waste water from potato processing plants, straw, molasses, animal manure and even sewage. It serves as a rich source of:
question.explanation: Microbes are being grown on an industrial scale as source of good protein. Microbes like Spirulina can be grown easily on materials like waste water from potato processing plants (containing starch), straw, molasses, animal manure and even sewage, to produce large quantities and can serve as food rich in protein, minerals, fats, carbohydrate and vitamins. This is known as Single Cell Protein (SCP).

question.text: A bacterial species, Methylophilus methylotrophus, because of its high rate of biomass production and growth, can be expected to produce roughly how many tonnes of protein?
question.explanation: It has been calculated that a 250 Kg cow produces 200 g of protein per day. In the same period, 250g of a micro-organism like Methylophilus methylotrophus, because of its high rate of biomass production and growth, can be expected to produce 25 tonnes of protein.

question.text: The process of growing cells, tissues, or organs in an artificial nutrient medium under aseptic conditions is known as:
question.explanation: The whole plant could be regenerated from explants, i.e., any part of a plant taken out and grown in a test tube, under sterile conditions in special nutrient media. This capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency. This method of growing plants is called tissue culture.

question.text: Hidden hunger refers to the deficiency of which of the following in the diet?
question.explanation: More than 840 million people in the world do not have adequate food to meet their daily food and nutritional requirements. A far greater number - three billion people - suffer from micronutrient, protein and vitamin deficiencies or 'hidden hunger'.

question.text: In 2000, maize hybrids that had twice the amount of the amino acids ______ and ______ compared to existing maize hybrids were developed.
question.explanation: In 2000, maize hybrids that had twice the amount of the amino acids, lysine and tryptophan, compared to existing maize hybrids were developed. Wheat variety, Atlas 66, having a high protein content, has been used as a donor for improving cultivated wheat.

question.text: The Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI), New Delhi, has released several vegetable crops that are rich in vitamins and minerals. Which of the following is an example?
question.option: Vitamin A enriched carrots, spinach, pumpkin
Vitamin C enriched bitter gourd, bathua, mustard, tomato
Iron and calcium enriched spinach and bathua
question.explanation: The Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI), New Delhi has also released several vegetable crops that are rich in vitamins and minerals, e.g., Vitamin A enriched carrots, spinach, pumpkin; Vitamin C enriched bitter gourd, bathua, mustard, tomato; iron and calcium enriched spinach and bathua; and protein enriched beans – broad, lablab, French and garden peas.

question.text: Multiple Ovulation Embryo Transfer Technology (MOET) is a programme for herd improvement. In this method, a cow is administered hormones with FSH-like activity. This induces:
question.explanation: In this method, a cow is administered hormones, with FSH-like activity, to induce follicular maturation and super ovulation - instead of one egg, which they normally yield per cycle, they produce 6-8 eggs.

question.text: In MOET, after the fertilized eggs at the 8–32 cell stage are recovered non-surgically, they are transferred to:
question.explanation: The fertilised eggs at 8–32 cells stages, are recovered non-surgically and transferred to surrogate mothers. The genetic mother is available for another round of super ovulation.

question.text: Apiculture is the maintenance of hives of honeybees for the production of honey. Which is the most common species of honeybee reared in India?
question.explanation: Bee-keeping or apiculture is the maintenance of hives of honeybees for the production of honey. There are several species of honeybees which can be reared. Of these, the most common species is Apis indica.

question.text: The entire collection (of plants/seeds) having all the diverse alleles for all genes in a given crop is called:
question.explanation: The entire collection (of plants/seeds) having all the diverse alleles for all genes in a given crop is called germplasm collection.

question.text: During plant breeding, after superior recombinants have been selected and tested, they are multiplied and made available to farmers. This process is called:
question.option: Testing, release and commercialisation of new cultivars
question.explanation: Testing, release and commercialisation of new cultivars: The newly selected lines are evaluated for their yield and other agronomic traits of quality, disease resistance, etc. The material is then tested in farmer's fields, for at least three growing seasons. The material thus evaluated is compared with the best available local crop cultivar and is finally released as a variety.

question.text: During the period 1960 to 2000, wheat production in India increased from 11 million tonnes to:
question.explanation: During the period 1960 to 2000, wheat production increased from 11 million tonnes to 75 million tonnes while rice production went up from 35 million tonnes to 89.5 million tonnes. This was due to the development of semi-dwarf varieties of wheat and rice.

question.text: Which sugarcane species had thicker stems and higher sugar content but did not grow well in North India?
question.explanation: Tropical canes grown in south India Saccharum officinarum had thicker stems and higher sugar content but did not grow well in north India.

question.text: How did plant breeders successfully develop a sugarcane variety with high yield, thick stems, high sugar, and the ability to grow in North India?
question.option: By crossing Saccharum barberi and Saccharum officinarum
question.explanation: These two species were successfully crossed to get sugar cane varieties combining the desirable qualities of high yield, thick stems, high sugar and ability to grow in the sugar cane areas of north India.

question.text: A wide range of fungal, bacterial, and viral pathogens affect the yield of cultivated crop species. Which of the following diseases is caused by a fungus?
question.explanation: Some of the diseases caused by fungi are rusts, e.g., brown rust of wheat, red rot of sugarcane and late blight of potato; by bacteria– black rot of crucifers; and by viruses– tobacco mosaic, turnip mosaic, etc.

question.text: Inducing mutations artificially and then selecting plants with desirable characters to use as a source in breeding is known as:
question.explanation: It is possible to induce mutations artificially through use of chemicals or radiations (like gamma radiations), and selecting and using the plants that have the desirable character as a source in breeding – this process is called mutation breeding.

question.text: In mung bean, resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew were induced by:
question.explanation: In mung bean, resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew were induced by mutations.

question.text: Resistance to yellow mosaic virus in bhindi (Abelmoschus esculentus) was transferred from a wild species, resulting in a new variety called:
question.explanation: Resistance to yellow mosaic virus in bhindi (Abelmoschus esculentus) was transferred from a wild species and resulted in a new variety of A. esculentus called Parbhani kranti.

question.text: Diets lacking essential micronutrients like iron, vitamin A, iodine, and zinc increase the risk for disease and reduce lifespan. What is this hidden form of hunger called?
question.explanation: Diets lacking essential micronutrients – particularly iron, vitamin A, iodine and zinc – increase the risk for disease, reduce lifespan and reduce mental abilities. Over three billion people suffer from micronutrient, protein and vitamin deficiencies or 'hidden hunger' because they cannot afford to buy enough fruits, vegetables, legumes, fish and meat.

question.text: Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats is called:
question.explanation: Biofortification – breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats – is the most practical means to improve public health.

question.text: In 2000, maize hybrids were developed that had twice the amount of which two essential amino acids compared to existing hybrids?
question.explanation: In 2000, maize hybrids that had twice the amount of the amino acids, lysine and tryptophan, compared to existing maize hybrids were developed.

question.text: Microbes are being grown on an industrial scale as a source of good protein. Which blue-green alga can be grown easily on materials like waste water from potato processing plants, straw, molasses, animal manure, and even sewage, to produce large quantities of food rich in protein, minerals, fats, carbohydrate and vitamins?
question.explanation: Microbes are being grown on an industrial scale as source of good protein. Blue-green algae like Spirulina can be grown easily on materials like waste water from potato processing plants... to produce large quantities and can serve as food rich in protein, minerals, fats, carbohydrate and vitamins.

question.text: It has been calculated that a 250 Kg cow produces 200 g of protein per day. In the same period, 250g of a micro-organism like Methylophilus methylotrophus can be expected to produce how much protein?
question.explanation: It has been calculated that a 250 Kg cow produces 200 g of protein per day. In the same period, 250g of a micro-organism like Methylophilus methylotrophus, because of its high rate of biomass production and growth, can be expected to produce 25 tonnes of protein.

question.text: Which of the following is a common fungus that many people eat, indicating that large-scale mushroom culture is a growing industry?
question.explanation: The fact that edible mushrooms are eaten by many people and large scale mushroom culture is a growing industry makes it believable that microscopic fungi too would become acceptable as food.
